statusLine: EU fuel security remains on watch despite adequate average reserves, with nine member states critically below safety thresholds amid ongoing Middle East supply disruptions.
fullAnalysis:
The EU's overall fuel security picture shows mixed signals as of April 2026. While aggregate reserves remain above the 90-day minimum requirement (petrol 94.5 days, diesel 95.8 days, jet fuel 89.9 days), this masks severe vulnerabilities in individual member states. Nine countries face critical fuel shortages, with Romania showing the most alarming deficits: just 20.2 days of diesel reserves and 43.6 days of petrol—less than half the required safety threshold.

The widespread nature of these shortfalls is particularly concerning. Major economies including Italy (69.5 days petrol, 64.3 days diesel), France (64.7 days petrol), and Germany (63.9 days diesel) all show critical deficiencies in key fuel categories. Eastern European nations are especially vulnerable, with Poland, Romania, and the Baltic states showing multiple critical shortages. This suggests the recent strategic reserve releases in response to Middle East supply disruptions have not been evenly distributed across the bloc.

Oil markets show some relief with Brent crude falling 4.98% to $107.16 per barrel, though prices remain elevated at 27% above pre-conflict levels. Consumer fuel prices reflect this volatility, with significant variations across the EU—from €1.34 per liter in Malta to €2.35 in the Netherlands for petrol. The current situation requires immediate attention to prevent localized fuel shortages from escalating into broader supply chain disruptions.
keyPoints: Nine EU member states face critical fuel shortages below the mandatory 90-day minimum, including major economies like Italy, France, and Germany
Romania shows the most severe crisis with only 20.2 days of diesel reserves—80% below the safety threshold
Recent strategic reserve releases have created uneven distribution, with Eastern Europe particularly vulnerable across all fuel types
Brent crude prices fell 4.98% to $107.16/barrel but remain 27% above pre-conflict levels, indicating continued market volatility
Consumer fuel prices vary dramatically across the EU, from €1.34/L in Malta to €2.35/L in Netherlands, reflecting supply chain stress
